subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 5! In this unit you will become an expert on two-dimensional shapes – the flat shapes that surround you every single day. Two-dimensional simply means the shape has length and width but no thickness, like a drawing on a page. When Amina looks at the rectangular door of the masjid, when Yusuf spins the round wheel of his bicycle in Hargeisa, or when Halima arranges square tiles on a kitchen floor, they are all looking at 2D shapes at work.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 6 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Identify and classify the seven special quadrilaterals by their properties, not just their appearance.
Describe the sides, angles, parallel sides and lines of symmetry of each quadrilateral.
Explain the relationships between quadrilaterals (for example, why every square is also a rectangle).
State that the four angles of any quadrilateral add up to 360°, and use this fact.
Name the parts of a circle: centre, radius, diameter, circumference and chord.
Use the rule diameter = 2 × radius to solve problems, and convert between cm and mm.
Draw accurate circles with a compass, and recognise the symmetry of shapes.
Apply shape knowledge to real-world problems in design, building and everyday life.

concepts.title: The Quadrilateral Family
concepts.explanation: A quadrilateral is any shape with exactly four straight sides and four angles. The word comes from Latin: *quadri* means four and *lateral* means sides. Think of quadrilaterals as one large family. Just as the members of your own family can look very different – some tall, some short – while still being related, quadrilaterals can look very different from each other while all sharing the same feature: four straight sides. In mathematics we pay special attention to seven special quadrilaterals, because they appear…
concepts.example: A quadrilateral is any shape with exactly four straight sides and four angles. The word comes from Latin: *quadri* means four and *lateral* means sides. Think of quadrilaterals as one large family. Just as the members…

concepts.title: Square, Rectangle, Parallelogram and Rhombus
concepts.explanation: A square is perhaps the most familiar quadrilateral. Think of a chessboard, a window pane, or a floor tile. A square has four equal sides – all exactly the same length – and four right angles, each measuring 90°. It has two pairs of parallel sides: the top and bottom are parallel, and the left and right are parallel. Its two diagonals are equal, they cut each other exactly in half (we say they bisect each other), and they cross at right angles. A square has four lines of symmetry: one vertical, one horizontal, and…
concepts.example: A parallelogram is like a rectangle that has been gently pushed over so it leans to one side. Its name gives away its main property: it has two pairs of parallel sides. The opposite sides are equal in length, and the…

concepts.title: Trapezium, Isosceles Trapezium and Kite
concepts.explanation: A trapezium (called a *trapezoid* in some countries) is a quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides. Think of the side view of a house roof, or the side panel of a slide in a playground. The two parallel sides are called the bases; the other two sides are not parallel and can be any length. The angles can vary widely, and a general trapezium has no lines of symmetry. Its diagonals have no special properties. An isosceles trapezium is a balanced, tidy version of the trapezium. Like every trapezium it…
concepts.example: A kite is named after the toy that flies in the sky over the beaches of Berbera. It has two pairs of adjacent equal sides – that is, two sides next to each other are equal, and the other two sides next to each other are…

concepts.title: How the Quadrilaterals Are Related
concepts.explanation: The seven quadrilaterals are not seven separate islands – they are connected like a family tree. Understanding these links is one of the most important ideas in the whole unit, because it explains why a shape can have more than one correct name. All squares are rectangles, because a square has four right angles (which is what makes a rectangle).
concepts.example: All squares are rhombuses, because a square has four equal sides (which is what makes a rhombus).

concepts.title: Lines of Symmetry
concepts.explanation: A line of symmetry is like a mirror line. If you fold a shape along a line of symmetry, the two halves match perfectly; and if you place a mirror on the line, the reflection completes the shape exactly. Different quadrilaterals have different numbers of lines of symmetry, and knowing them is a favourite exam question. 2 through the sides, 2 through the diagonals
concepts.example: Down the middle, between the parallel sides

concepts.title: Tessellation and Decomposing Shapes
concepts.explanation: Tessellation means fitting shapes together with no gaps and no overlaps, like the pieces of a jigsaw or the tiles on a floor. Many quadrilaterals tessellate, which is exactly why they are chosen for floors and walls. In fact, every quadrilateral can tessellate. The reason is beautiful: the four angles of a quadrilateral add to 360°, and the angles that meet around any single point must also add to 360° – so four copies of the same quadrilateral, arranged corner to corner, fill the space around a point perfectly.…
concepts.example: Decomposing a shape means breaking it into smaller, simpler shapes. This is a powerful problem-solving skill. Any quadrilateral can be split into two triangles by drawing a single diagonal from one corner to the…

methods.title: How to classify a quadrilateral
methods.example: A shape has four equal sides and four right angles. What is its most specific name?
steps: Count the pairs of parallel sides.
Measure the sides to see which are equal.
Check the angles for right angles.
Count the lines of symmetry by folding, then give the most specific name.

methods.title: How to find a missing angle
methods.example: Three angles of a quadrilateral are 100°, 80° and 90°. What is the fourth angle?
steps: Add the three angles you know.
Subtract that total from 360°.
The answer is the fourth angle.

methods.title: How to draw a circle with a compass
methods.example: A kite has one pair of sides 6 cm each and the other pair 8 cm each. What is its perimeter?
steps: Open the compass to the radius against a ruler.
Press the sharp point firmly on the centre.
Turn the pencil arm all the way round, keeping the opening the same.

methods.title: Radius from diameter
methods.example: A bicycle wheel has a diameter of 50 cm. What is its radius?
steps: Solution: Use r = D ÷ 2.
So radius = 50 ÷ 2 = 25 cm.
The radius is always half the diameter.

methods.title: Diameter from radius, with a real object
methods.example: A round clock face in the Nuur Academy hall has a radius of 15 cm. What is its diameter, and how long is the hour hand that reaches from the centre to the edge?
steps: Solution: Diameter = 2 × r = 2 × 15 = 30 cm.
The hour hand reaches from the centre to the edge, which is exactly the radius, so it is 15 cm long.
Check the diameter from radius, with a real object result carefully.

methods.title: Lines of symmetry
methods.example: How many lines of symmetry does a rectangle that is not a square have?
steps: Solution: A rectangle that is not a square has 2 lines of symmetry – one horizontal through the middle and one vertical through the middle.
Its diagonals are not lines of symmetry, which you can check by folding.
Check the lines of symmetry result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Name the shape
workedExamples.prompt: A shape has four equal sides and four right angles. What is its most specific name?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Four right angles make it a rectangle; four equal sides make it a rhombus. A shape that is both at once is a square. Always give the most specific name, so the answer is *square*, not just rectangle.

workedExamples.title: The missing angle
workedExamples.prompt: Three angles of a quadrilateral are 100°, 80° and 90°. What is the fourth angle?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The four angles of any quadrilateral add up to 360°. Add the three known angles: 100 + 80 + 90 = 270°. Subtract from 360: 360 − 270 = 90°. The fourth angle is 90°.

workedExamples.title: Perimeter of a kite
workedExamples.prompt: A kite has one pair of sides 6 cm each and the other pair 8 cm each. What is its perimeter?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A kite has two pairs of adjacent equal sides, so the four sides are 6, 6, 8 and 8. Add them: 6 + 6 + 8 + 8 = 28 cm.

workedExamples.title: Radius from diameter
workedExamples.prompt: A bicycle wheel has a diameter of 50 cm. What is its radius?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use r = D ÷ 2. So radius = 50 ÷ 2 = 25 cm. The radius is always half the diameter.

workedExamples.title: Diameter from radius, with a real object
workedExamples.prompt: A round clock face in the Nuur Academy hall has a radius of 15 cm. What is its diameter, and how long is the hour hand that reaches from the centre to the edge?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Diameter = 2 × r = 2 × 15 = 30 cm. The hour hand reaches from the centre to the edge, which is exactly the radius, so it is 15 cm long.

workedExamples.title: Lines of symmetry
workedExamples.prompt: How many lines of symmetry does a rectangle that is not a square have?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A rectangle that is not a square has 2 lines of symmetry – one horizontal through the middle and one vertical through the middle. Its diagonals are not lines of symmetry, which you can check by folding.

workedExamples.title: Unit conversion
workedExamples.prompt: A circle has a radius of 4 cm. What is its diameter in millimetres?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First find the diameter in cm: D = 2 × 4 = 8 cm. Then convert: 1 cm = 10 mm, so 8 cm = 80 mm. Always convert after finding the diameter, and keep the units consistent.

workedExamples.title: A square inside a circle
workedExamples.prompt: A square is drawn inside a circle so that all four corners touch the circle. The circle has a radius of 5 cm. What is the length of the square's diagonal?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The diagonal of the square passes straight through the centre from one corner to the opposite corner – so it is the diameter of the circle. Diameter = 2 × radius = 2 × 5 = 10 cm. So the square's diagonal is 10 cm.

workedExamples.title: Which of these are always true for a rhombus
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these are always true for a rhombus? a) four equal sides b) four right angles c) two lines of symmetry d) opposite sides parallel.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Copy and complete: 'Every square is a ___ and a ___, but not every rectangle is a ___.'
workedExamples.solution: a, c and d are always true; b is not (a rhombus need not have right angles).

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: A compass is opened so its points are 4 cm apart. What is the radius and the diameter of the circle it will draw?
workedExamples.solution: 'Every square is a rectangle and a rhombus, but not every rectangle is a square.'

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A quadrilateral has opposite sides parallel, all sides equal and all angles equal. Give its most specific name and explain.
workedExamples.solution: 1) A square – opposite sides parallel makes it a parallelogram, all sides equal makes it a rhombus, all angles equal (90°) makes it a rectangle; a shape that is all three is a square.

practice.answer: 1) A square – opposite sides parallel makes it a parallelogram, all sides equal makes it a rhombus, all angles equal (90°) makes it a rectangle; a shape that is all three is a square.
practice.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

activities.title: Quadrilateral Detective
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, a pencil, colouring pens.
steps: Make a table with columns: Shape, Equal sides, Right angles, Parallel pairs, Lines of symmetry.
Draw each of the seven special quadrilaterals as neatly as you can with your ruler.
Mark equal sides with little ticks, right angles with small squares, and parallel sides with arrows.
Fill in your table for all seven shapes, then look for patterns (which has the most symmetry?).
Keep your detective table – it becomes your personal revision chart for the whole unit.

activities.title: Shape Hunt Around the Home
activities.materials: a notebook and pencil (a phone camera is optional).
steps: Walk through your home and find at least three real examples of each quadrilateral.
Then hunt for circles: wheels, plates, coins, clocks, the bottom of a cup.
For each object, write its shape and why that shape suits its job (why are doors rectangles?).
Which shape did you find most often? Why do you think builders and makers choose it so often?

activities.title: The Folding Symmetry Test
steps: Carefully cut out a square, a rectangle, a rhombus, a kite and a parallelogram.
Fold each shape in every way you can to find the fold lines where the halves match exactly.
Count the lines of symmetry for each shape and write them down.
Try to fold a parallelogram along its diagonal – do the halves match? Explain what you discover.
Folding proves symmetry far better than guessing – trust your hands!

activities.title: Compass Circle Workshop
activities.materials: a compass (or string and a pencil), a ruler, paper.
steps: Set your compass to 2 cm and draw a circle. Repeat for 3.5 cm, 5 cm and 7.5 cm.
For each circle, measure the diameter across the centre and record it beside the radius.
Check the rule: is every diameter exactly twice its radius? Write what you notice.
Draw a set of circles all sharing one centre (concentric circles) and colour the rings.

activities.title: Tessellation Tiles (Family Task)
activities.materials: card or thick paper, scissors, colouring pens, and a helper.
steps: Cut out about 12 identical copies of one quadrilateral (start with a parallelogram).
Fit them together on a large sheet with no gaps and no overlaps – that is a tessellation.
Colour the pattern so it looks like a real tiled floor from a market or masjid.
Now try a kite or a trapezium. Which shapes tessellate most easily, and why?

activities.title: The Shape Riddle Game
activities.materials: paper number/shape cards and a partner.
steps: Each player writes five riddles, such as 'I have four equal sides but no right angles – what am I?'
Swap riddles with your partner and try to name each mystery shape.
Award one point for each correct shape and explanation.
Make a harder round using circles: 'I am the longest chord – what am I?' (a diameter).

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand shape properties best? What surprised you about symmetry or tessellation? Which shape is still tricky to name? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Quadrilateral Angle Rule
rules.text: The four angles of ANY quadrilateral add up to 360 degrees. Find a missing angle by subtracting the other three from 360.

rules.title: Radius and Diameter Rule
rules.text: Diameter = 2 x radius. Radius = diameter / 2. The diameter is the longest chord and passes through the centre.

rules.title: Classification Rule
rules.text: Every square is a rectangle and a rhombus; a square is a special parallelogram. Always give the MOST specific correct name, and judge by properties, not by how the shape is turned.

terms: A shape with four straight sides
Sides that never meet, always the same distance apart
A fold line where the two halves match
Centre-to-edge / right across through the centre
A line joining two points on a circle
Tiling with no gaps and no overlaps
commonMistakes: It is still a square – check sides and angles
Saying a square is not a rectangle
A square has 4 right angles, so it IS a rectangle
A general parallelogram has 0 lines of symmetry

realProblems.prompt: A soccer pitch in Kismaayo has rectangular goal areas 18 m wide and 5.5 m deep. What quadrilateral is a goal area, and list two of its properties.
realProblems.answer: 1) A rectangle; opposite sides equal and parallel, all four angles 90°, 2 lines of symmetry (any two).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) A rectangle; opposite sides equal and parallel, all four angles 90°, 2 lines of symmetry (any two).

realProblems.prompt: Maryam plants a circular garden with a radius of 3 m. How wide is the garden across the middle (its diameter)?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A bicycle wheel has a radius of 35 cm. When it makes one full turn, the distance the bicycle rolls forward equals which part of the circle? (You do not need to calculate it.)
realProblems.answer: It equals the circumference of the wheel.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: It equals the circumference of the wheel.

realProblems.prompt: A flag for Eid has a rhombus in its centre with all sides 20 cm. What is its perimeter, and how is a rhombus different from a square?
realProblems.answer: Perimeter = 4 × 20 = 80 cm; a rhombus has four equal sides like a square, but its angles are not usually right angles.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Perimeter = 4 × 20 = 80 cm; a rhombus has four equal sides like a square, but its angles are not usually right angles.

realProblems.prompt: Explain, using circle properties, why manhole covers are made round rather than square.
realProblems.answer: A round cover cannot fall through its own opening in any position, because a circle's width is the same in every direction; a square cover could be turned diagonally and drop through.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A round cover cannot fall through its own opening in any position, because a circle's width is the same in every direction; a square cover could be turned diagonally and drop through.

realProblems.prompt: A quadrilateral has opposite sides parallel, all sides equal and all angles equal. Give its most specific name and explain.
realProblems.answer: 1) A square – opposite sides parallel makes it a parallelogram, all sides equal makes it a rhombus, all angles equal (90°) makes it a rectangle; a shape that is all three is a square.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) A square – opposite sides parallel makes it a parallelogram, all sides equal makes it a rhombus, all angles equal (90°) makes it a rectangle; a shape that is all three is a square.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A quadrilateral has opposite sides parallel, all sides equal and all angles equal. Give its most specific name and explain.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) A square – opposite sides parallel makes it a parallelogram, all sides equal makes it a rhombus, all angles equal (90°) makes it a rectangle; a shape that is all three is a square.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: An isosceles trapezium has parallel sides of 10 cm and 6 cm and legs of 5 cm each. What makes it 'isosceles', and how many lines of symmetry does it have?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: It is 'isosceles' because its two non-parallel sides (legs) are equal; it has 1 line of symmetry, straight down the middle.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Two circles have the same centre. The inner radius is 4 cm and the outer radius is 7 cm. What is the gap between the two edges?
A square is inscribed in a circle of radius 10 cm (all four corners touch the circle). What is the length of the square's diagonal? Explain your reasoning.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The diagonal passes through the centre corner-to-corner, so it is the diameter = 2 × 10 = 20 cm.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Write a systematic 4-step method that would let you classify any unknown quadrilateral.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: For example: (1) count the sides and check they are straight; (2) count pairs of parallel sides; (3) measure the sides to see which are equal; (4) check the angles for right angles; then give the most specific name.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Tessellation and Decomposing Shapes.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Tessellation means fitting shapes together with no gaps and no overlaps, like the pieces of a jigsaw or the tiles on a floor. Many quadrilaterals tessellate, which is exactly why they are chosen for floors and walls. In fact, every quadrilateral can tessellate. The reason is beautiful: the four angles of a quadrilateral add to 360°, and the angles that meet around any single point must also add to 360° – so four copies of the same quadrilateral, arranged corner to corner, fill the space around a point perfectly.…

questions.question: What does “Quadrilateral” mean?
options: A shape with four straight sides
Sides that never meet, always the same distance apart
A fold line where the two halves match
questions.answer: A shape with four straight sides
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Quadrilateral means A shape with four straight sides.

questions.question: What does “Parallel” mean?
options: Sides that never meet, always the same distance apart
A fold line where the two halves match
Centre-to-edge / right across through the centre
questions.answer: Sides that never meet, always the same distance apart
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Parallel means Sides that never meet, always the same distance apart.

selfAssessment: I can identify and classify the seven special quadrilaterals by their properties, not just their appearance.
I can describe the sides, angles, parallel sides and lines of symmetry of each quadrilateral.
I can explain the relationships between quadrilaterals (for example, why every square is also a rectangle).
I can state that the four angles of any quadrilateral add up to 360°, and use this fact.
I can name the parts of a circle: centre, radius, diameter, circumference and chord.
I can use the rule diameter = 2 × radius to solve problems, and convert between cm and mm.
I can draw accurate circles with a compass, and recognise the symmetry of shapes.
I can apply shape knowledge to real-world problems in design, building and everyday life.
